title: Section 7 Alterations in Renal and Urinary Tract Function

items.subtopic: Section 7 Alterations in Renal and Urinary Tract Function | Interstitial Cystitis/Bladder Pain Syndrome | DEFINITION
items.title: Understanding the Definition of IC/BPS
items.content: Interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome (IC/BPS) is defined by chronic bladder-associated pain, urinary storage symptoms like frequency and urgency, negative urine cultures, and absence of identifiable causes. The condition's definition has evolved to emphasize pain perceived to be related to the bladder with lower urinary tract symptoms lasting more than 6 weeks.
keyPoints: IC/BPS is characterized by chronic nature and bladder-related pain with LUTS.
Pain may also occur outside the bladder, including in the pelvis, perineum, genitals, abdomen.
Definition includes an unpleasant sensation related to the bladder lasting >6 weeks without infection or other identifiable causes.

items.subtopic: Section 7 Alterations in Renal and Urinary Tract Function | Interstitial Cystitis/Bladder Pain Syndrome | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Etiology and Pathogenesis of IC/BPS
items.content: The etiology of interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome (IC/BPS) remains uncertain, with no single cause identified. It is considered a syndrome involving multiple interrelated disease processes that extend beyond the bladder.
keyPoints: No definitive single etiology for IC/BPS has been established.
IC/BPS likely represents a constellation of interrelated disease processes.
The condition manifests in a spectrum reaching beyond just the bladder.

items.subtopic: Section 7 Alterations in Renal and Urinary Tract Function | Interstitial Cystitis/Bladder Pain Syndrome | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | INFECTION AND THE URINARY MICROBIOTA
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome (IC/BPS) as accepted by major international groups?
options: It causes pain perceived to be attributable to the bladder.
Pain outside the bladder, such as in the pelvis or abdomen, is common.
items.explanation: Hunner lesions are not a necessary characteristic of IC/BPS; they were part of earlier definitions but are not required for contemporary diagnosis.
items.reference: Section 7 Alterations in Renal and Urinary Tract Function · Section 7 Alterations in Renal and Urinary Tract Function | Interstitial Cystitis/Bladder Pain Syndrome | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | INFECTION AND THE URINARY MICROBIOTA

items.subtopic: Section 7 Alterations in Renal and Urinary Tract Function | Interstitial Cystitis/Bladder Pain Syndrome | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | INFECTION AND THE URINARY MICROBIOTA
items.question: What is the contemporary definition of interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome (IC/BPS) according to major international groups?
options: An acute condition with bladder inflammation and positive urine cultures.
A chronic condition causing pain perceived to be attributable to the bladder, associated with urinary symptoms for >6 weeks in the absence of infection or other identifiable causes.
A syndrome characterized by Hunner lesions and negative urine cultures only.
A condition involving bacterial infection of the urothelium.
items.explanation: The contemporary definition includes chronic pain perceived to be related to the bladder, associated with urinary symptoms for more than six weeks without infection or other identifiable causes.
items.reference: Section 7 Alterations in Renal and Urinary Tract Function · Section 7 Alterations in Renal and Urinary Tract Function | Interstitial Cystitis/Bladder Pain Syndrome | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | INFECTION AND THE URINARY MICROBIOTA

items.subtopic: Section 7 Alterations in Renal and Urinary Tract Function | Interstitial Cystitis/Bladder Pain Syndrome | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | INFECTION AND THE URINARY MICROBIOTA
items.question: Which statement best describes the relationship between IC/BPS and CP/CPPS?
options: IC/BPS is a subset of CP/CPPS.
CP/CPPS can exist only in women, while IC/BPS affects both men and women.
IC/BPS and CP/CPPS are distinct disorders but may have overlapping symptoms and shared pathophysiology.
Both conditions are caused by bacterial infections of the urothelium.
items.explanation: IC/BPS can exist independently or alongside CP/CPPS, with both having potential overlapping symptom presentations and possibly sharing etiologic origins.

items.subtopic: Section 7 Alterations in Renal and Urinary Tract Function | Interstitial Cystitis/Bladder Pain Syndrome | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | INFECTION AND THE URINARY MICROBIOTA
items.statement: A single etiology for IC/BPS has been definitively identified.
items.explanation: The search for a singular cause of IC/BPS has not succeeded, and it is considered more likely to be a syndrome with multiple interrelated disease processes.
items.reference: Section 7 Alterations in Renal and Urinary Tract Function · Section 7 Alterations in Renal and Urinary Tract Function | Interstitial Cystitis/Bladder Pain Syndrome | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | INFECTION AND THE URINARY MICROBIOTA

items.subtopic: Section 7 Alterations in Renal and Urinary Tract Function | Interstitial Cystitis/Bladder Pain Syndrome | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | INFECTION AND THE URINARY MICROBIOTA
items.statement: Bacterial infection of the urothelium is definitively shown to cause IC/BPS.
items.explanation: While bacterial infection has been considered a suspect in the etiology, it has never been definitively proven to cause IC/BPS.

items.subtopic: Section 7 Alterations in Renal and Urinary Tract Function | Interstitial Cystitis/Bladder Pain Syndrome | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | INFECTION AND THE URINARY MICROBIOTA
items.reason: The disease manifests with persistent symptoms over time, as reflected in the accepted definitions by major international groups.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why IC/BPS is considered chronic.
